content.full_text:
State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
: Advice Request Meeting Participants 
Dote 
Sept. 4, 1984 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Carla Wardlow 
Subject: Section 86205 (a) --Loan to Legislator 
A lobbyist is selling his residence in Sacramento and 
a legislator wants to purchase it. 
May the lobbyist 
take back a note on the sale? Section 86205(a) prohibits 
a lobbyist from doing anything with the purpose of placing 
any elected state officer, etc., under personal obliga-
tion to him or to his employer. 
In the Reinhardt opinion (3 FPPC Ops. 83, No. 76-091), 
the Commission said that an arrangement between a lobbying 
firm and a state candidate to provide management or 
consultant services in exchange for full and adequate 
consideration "is not the type of arrangement at which 
the Section is directed. 
The arrangement does not involve 
an attempt by the firm or its employees to pervert the 
normal legislative or administrative processes by means 
of some illegitimate activity .•.. " 


State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
Advice File: 
Sections 82036, 84206 
Date 
November 7, 1984 
No • .r-1-84-312 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Carla Wardlow 
Subject: 
Loan Forgiveness During Late Contribution Period 
Senator Garamendi received a loan during 1982 which was for-
given during the 1984 general election late contribution 
period. 
Must the forgiveness be reported as a "late contribu-
tion?" 
Per Barbara, the forgiveness would not trigger a late contri-
bution report. 
However, this advice might be different if 
a payment due during the late contribution period were 
forgiven. 


State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
Advice File: 
Section 82028 
82045 
18623 
18728 
Dote 
Nov. 7, 1984 
No. M-84-313 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Carla Wardlow 
Subject: 
Reporting Air Transportation Received in Connection 
with Speaking Engagements 
An official who receives free intrastate transportation in 
connection with an event at which he or she speaks, partici-
pates in a panel, etc., has not received a gift so long as 
the transportation is provided by or arranged by the sponsor 
of the event. 
If the official is required to provide his or 
her own transportation and arranges for a free ride from some 
other source, a gift has been received.

sections.question_synthetic: This document contains three separate FPPC advice memoranda addressing: (1) whether a lobbyist may take back a promissory note when selling residential property to a legislator under Section 86205(a); (2) whether loan forgiveness during a late contribution period must be reported as a late contribution under Sections 82036 and 84206; and (3) whether free intrastate transportation provided in connection with speaking engagements constitutes a reportable gift under Sections 82028, 82045, 18623, and 18728.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that: (1) a lobbyist taking back a note on a residence sale to a legislator may not violate Section 86205(a) if it represents a normal commercial transaction without intent to place the officer under personal obligation; (2) loan forgiveness occurring during a late contribution period would not automatically trigger a late contribution report, though the analysis differs if a payment due during that period were forgiven; and (3) free intrastate transportation provided by or arranged by an event sponsor is not a reportable gift, but transportation arranged independently by the official from other sources constitutes a gift requiring reporting.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains three separate memoranda without traditional Q/A section headers; questions and conclusions were synthesized from the subject lines and body text; first memorandum references prior opinion (Reinhardt) rather than providing explicit FPPC conclusion; second and third memoranda provide clear advisory conclusions; extracted three distinct legal questions and corresponding FPPC positions

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: This document contains three separate FPPC advice memoranda addressing: (1) whether a lobbyist may take back a promissory note when selling residential property to a legislator under Section 86205(a); (2) whether loan forgiveness during a late contribution period must be reported as a late contribution under Sections 82036 and 84206; and (3) whether free intrastate transportation provided in connection with speaking engagements constitutes a reportable gift under Sections 82028, 82045, 18623, and 18728.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC concludes that: (1) a lobbyist taking back a note on a residence sale to a legislator may not violate Section 86205(a) if it represents a normal commercial transaction without intent to place the officer under personal obligation; (2) loan forgiveness occurring during a late contribution period would not automatically trigger a late contribution report, though the analysis differs if a payment due during that period were forgiven; and (3) free intrastate transportation provided by or arranged by an event sponsor is not a reportable gift, but transportation arranged independently by the official from other sources constitutes a gift requiring reporting.

embedding.summary: Three FPPC advice memoranda from 1984 addressing lobbying regulations, campaign finance reporting requirements for loan forgiveness, and gift reporting standards for transportation provided in connection with official speaking engagements.
